narrative: Your undergraduate GPA of 2.40 and your positions in the lower deciles for your Year 1 and Year 3 grades—specifically the 2nd decile early on and 1st decile by Year 3—stood out as key factors in the model's assessment of your law school application. While your LSAT score of 41 is stronger than the average for students who passed the bar exam on their first try, and your middle family income bracket aligns with a substantial portion of successful applicants, the model focused on patterns from past students to predict your bar exam outcome. It estimated a 61.9% probability that you would fail the bar on your first attempt, which crossed the threshold for denying admission since the school prioritizes applicants likely to pass and succeed in practice. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 decile ranking, as it was unusually low compared to the typical 5.91 average among those who passed, signaling potential challenges in sustaining strong academic performance. Next in importance, your solid LSAT score worked strongly in your favor, pulling the prediction toward success much like it does for many admitted students. Your Year 1 decile also contributed toward the failure outlook, though less forcefully, since it too fell well below the 6.04 average for bar passers. Coming from a middle-income background and planning full-time studies matches the profile of most successful students, but it couldn't outweigh the academic decile concerns here. Overall, your profile compares to accepted students by having a competitive LSAT edge, yet lagging in undergraduate and in-school grade deciles that have historically predicted bar struggles. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background—gender and race weren't factors in the decision—but a data-driven call based on these academic signals. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT if possible to build an even stronger case, or focus on boosting your GPA through targeted coursework or post-bac programs to shift those decile perceptions. You might also gather letters of recommendation highlighting your resilience and full-time commitment, then reapply to schools with holistic reviews that weigh improvement stories. With strategic steps, you can reshape how models and admissions teams see your trajectory.
